critique.summary: The article describes a GitHub repository by Andreas Jansson that archives tiled background .bmp files from Windows 3.1. The repository includes image files, scripts, and screenshots related to these classic backgrounds.
mainPoints: The repository archives Windows 3.1 tiled background bitmap images.
It contains folders for cropped images and screenshots.
There is a Python script named crop.py included in the repository.
The repository has 64 stars, 5 forks, and 2 watchers on GitHub.
No official releases or packages have been published.
critique.truthfulness: The claims about the repository content and its purpose are accurate based on the publicly available GitHub page. The repository indeed hosts Windows 3.1 background images and related files. There are no misleading or false claims present.
considerations: The article does not mention the licensing or usage rights of the archived images.
There is no information about the completeness or source verification of the Windows 3.1 backgrounds.
The repository appears to be maintained by a single contributor, which may affect long-term maintenance or updates.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion revolves around nostalgia for Windows 3.1 tiled backgrounds and related retro computing experiences. Commenters share personal memories, links to archives and resources, and reflections on the evolution of graphical user interfaces and hardware.

comments.author: malux85
comments.text: One of my hobbies is to install older versions of windows and play around with them a bit - at the moment I have 86Box emulating an old machine with windows 3.11 installed and Visual C++, and I'm writing a little implementaiton of DFT so I can simulate a water molecule. Mostly because I want to go back to the days of when I had a 486 in my bedroom, I finally got a graphical desktop working, and the feeling of joy with the old wallpapers, the clock running, a coding IDE, I spent hours in there learning about memory allocations, functions, OpenGL, such good memories that turned into a lifelong career.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a detailed, plausible personal experience with retro computing and software development, adding valuable context and authenticity to the nostalgia theme.

comments.author: OCTAGRAM
comments.text:
Tiles were comparably slow to draw. Windows 3.1 and maybe more recent Windows had a background option called "patterns". These are 8x8 patterns of black-over-something. Second color is customizable. It could be white, but also something else. EGA/VGA hardware specific is that 8 pixel wide repeating stuff can be drawn relatively quickly. Patterns were not inside files. They are probably inside registry? Or ini files?

Whatever. The end result is that patterns are forgotten and omitted from retro background collections.
comments.analysis: The comment offers technical insight into Windows 3.1 background rendering, explaining patterns and their implementation, which aligns with known facts about early Windows graphics.

comments.author: t1234s
comments.text: back in the 3.11/95 era when doing troubleshooting of slow PC's I would always change the bitmap wallpaper to a solid color. nothing more painful then watching a slow machine waste resources trying to render a desktop background.
comments.analysis: This comment accurately reflects common performance optimization practices from the era, showing practical knowledge of system resource management on older Windows versions.

comments.author: LetsGetTechnicl
comments.text:
Here's someone's personal archive of weird miscellanea, including old Windows wallpapers which is what reminded me. I use unironically use the classic Packard Bell tile background on my computers because it reminds me of my grandmother's PC which is one of the first I ever used.

https://www.dvd3000.ca/wp/extra/pb.html
comments.analysis: The comment shares a relevant personal anecdote and a link to an archive, enriching the discussion with a direct resource and nostalgic context.

comments.author: patrickscoleman
comments.text:
we have a crt tv with built in vhs in our office and have a bunch of old tapes

one is “Mastering Windows 3.1”

it’s fun to run in the background while working

for your enjoyment, here’s a similar 3.1 tutorial video from that era uploaded to YouTube

https://youtu.be/KRi5mjMgORk?si=OFH7UhOQif5EUCtg
comments.analysis: This comment adds a multimedia dimension to the nostalgia by referencing period-specific tutorial videos, which is plausible and relevant.

comments.author: BobMcBob
comments.text:
I know this isnt related to the post, but does anyone remember the artist or website that had a bunch of cool textures and colorful tiled wallpaper for the early days of linux? think mid-90s.

I've been scratching my head for years and my searches have never found it, but there has to be some white beards here that can recall it.

I remember there were some really cool options available, I believe in a square format for better tiling. If anyone can remember and post a link or archive I would very much appreciate it.
comments.analysis: The comment is a genuine inquiry about early Linux wallpapers, showing engagement but lacking direct information or verification.

comments.author: mikestorrent
comments.text: the 256color.bmp looks wrong, it's supposed to look more 3d, because it actually does use the 256 color pallette
comments.analysis: This technical observation about the color palette and visual appearance of a wallpaper file is plausible but lacks supporting detail.

comments.author: canyp
comments.text: Peak Windows. Everything went downhill from there.
comments.analysis: This is a subjective and emotional opinion without supporting evidence, reflecting nostalgia but not contributing factual information.

comments.author: soopypoos
comments.text: Last night I dreamed of flying toasters
comments.analysis: This comment is a humorous, nostalgic reference but adds no substantive content to the discussion.

comments.author: thowme923874
comments.text:
Love this. Do it again, but for the whole package history of propaganda-debian.

Edit: I guess the package still exists, even though many desktop refuse to tile jpg. :/
comments.analysis: The comment expresses enthusiasm and references a Debian package, but the connection to the main topic is tangential and somewhat unclear.
